title: Latent-domain experts instead of one global model with post-hoc regime weights
summary: Domain-generalization work with mixture-of-experts argues that heterogeneous data benefit from expert specialization and learned routing, especially when true domains are hidden.
rationale: Domain-generalization work with mixture-of-experts argues that heterogeneous data benefit from expert specialization and learned routing, especially when true domains are hidden. This dataset clearly mixes dry/wet compounds, testing vs race sessions, different seasons, and track-energy regimes; current blending adjusts predictions by regime after training, but the base learners themselves remain global.
implementation_hint: Cluster rows or race-groups using adversarial-validation features or simple latent-domain descriptors, then train separate experts for 3-5 domains plus one global fallback. Use an OOF gate based on regime features and domain score to combine experts, with minimum-sample guards so wet/testing domains back off to the global model.
expected_effect: Should improve robustness under train-test shift and produce a more diverse ensemble member than another small feature tweak to the global XGB/Cat pair.
risk: Expert fragmentation can hurt rare regimes and make CV noisy; the gate can overfit unless built from strictly OOF expert predictions.
